interest: This argument is raised on behalf of low-SES and Roma families in segregated primaries who lack access to test-prep, seeking to expose that the central written exam functions as a preparation-wealth filter rather than an ability filter.
value: Equity vs. meritocratic legibility — the claim pits fair access for the under-resourced against the assumption that a standardised written exam objectively measures ability.
fear: The fear is that renaming or reweighting the exam legitimises the status quo as 'reformed' while the real sorting mechanism — preparation inequality bought by tutoring — remains untouched, leaving disadvantaged children no better off.
affected: families who can afford private tutoring
selective schools and their admissions gatekeepers
assumption: The claim rests on the unstated assumption that a genuinely less-coachable assessment instrument exists or can be built, and that current exam performance is substantially driven by paid preparation rather than underlying ability.
empirical_uncertainty: Contested and partly unknown: the scenario itself flags that no validated less-coachable instrument exists and that the degree to which exam scores reflect coaching versus ability is not settled by published psychometric data in this system.
